title: Data Science Lab
text: This site is a collection of small experiments exploring data science with R.

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
The resurgence of people moving to downtown Cleveland is making news.1 According to a study commissioned by Downtown Cleveland Inc., the downtown population was almost 19,000 in the 2020 census, a 22% increase from 2010.2 However, Cleveland Open Data shows only 13,0003. Cleveland Scene reports that there are lots of estimates out there, one as low as 8,000!4 What gives? The organizations may be using different sources, like the decennial US census vs the more recent, but less comprehensive, American Community Survey. But it seems more likely they are using different geographic boundaries.
I was able to reproduce some estimates. My main tools to do this were the tidycensus R package for US Census data, and the Cleveland Open Data service for Cleveland neighborhood definitions. I’ll step through the process below.

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
2020
The decennial census developer page has several data files for each census. Through trial and error, I discovered Redistricting Data (PL 94-171) contains overall population. There is a full list of variables that represent the various sub-groups of the population. I used it and the tidycensus::load_variables() function to identify the ones I want. I’ll include race/ethnicity to investigate demographic trends.

pl_2020_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2020, "pl") |>
  filter(
    between(name, "P2_001N", "P2_011N"),
    !name %in% c("P2_003N", "P2_004N")
  )

Here they are after a bit of cleaning. I created var_level to group several infrequent values into “Other”.


Show the code
pl_2020_vars <- 
  pl_2020_vars |>
  mutate(
    var_detail = case_when(
      str_detect(label, "White") ~ "White",
      str_detect(label, "Black") ~ "Black",
      str_detect(label, "Asian") ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(label, "American Indian") ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(label, "Native Hawaiian") ~ "Pacific Islander",
      str_detect(label, "Some Other Race") ~ "Some Other Race",
      str_detect(label, "two or more races") ~ "Two or more races",
      str_detect(label, "Hispanic") ~ "Hispanic",
      str_detect(label, "Total") ~ "Total",
      TRUE ~ label
    ),
    var_group = if_else(name == "P2_001N", "Total", "Race/ethnicity"),
    var_level = if_else(
      var_detail %in% c("White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Total"),
      var_detail, "Other")
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

The Demographic Profile contains age data.

dp_2020_vars <- 
  tidycensus::load_variables(2020, "dp") |>
  filter(
    str_detect(label, "Count!!SEX AND AGE!!Total population"),
    !str_detect(label, "Selected Age Categories"),
    name != "DP1_0001C"
  )

I’ll ignore sex and aggregate the ages into ten-year buckets.


Show the code
dp_2020_vars <- 
  dp_2020_vars |>
  mutate(
    var_detail = str_remove_all(label, "(Count!!SEX AND AGE!!Total population)|(!!)"),
    var_detail = if_else(var_detail == "", "Total", var_detail),
    var_group = "Age",
    var_level = case_when(
      name <= "DP1_0004C" ~ "Under 15 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0006C" ~ "15 to 24 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0008C" ~ "25 to 34 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0010C" ~ "35 to 44 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0012C" ~ "45 to 54 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0014C" ~ "55 to 64 yrs",
      TRUE ~ "65+ yrs"
    )
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

With the variables identified, request the data from the API. Cleveland is one of 59 subdivisions within Cuyahoga County. I’ll download the subdivision data to get a total count for Cuyahoga County and for Cleveland. Counties are composed of census tracts, and census tracts are composed of census blocks. Cities overlap census tracts, so I’ll download the block-level data and join to the cleve_blocks dataset from Cleveland Open Data. Urban Partners defined their Downtown Core and Westside areas by tract and some blocks from the Central SPA. I figured out which tracts and blocks by studying their map and swearing a lot.


Show the code
# Utility function to create factors
var_relevel <- function(x) {
  race_levels = c("Black", "White", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Other", "Total")
  ethn <- c("Black", "White", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Other", "Total")
  x <- fct_relevel(x, race_levels, after = Inf)
  x <- fct_relevel(x, "Under 15 yrs", after = 0)
  return(x)
}

# Urban Partners definitions of greater Cleveland uses tracts and blocks.
westside_tracts_2020 <- c(
  "103100", "103400", "103500", "103602", "103800", "103900", "104100", 
  "104200", "104300", "197800", "197700", "197500", "104400"
)
downtown_core_tracts_2020 <- c("103300", "107101", "107701", "107802", "109301")
downtown_core_blocks_2020 <- paste0("39035108701", c("2001", "2004", "2006", "2008"))

if (USE_API) {

  subdiv_2020_pl_detail <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "pl",
      variables = pl_2020_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE, 
      year = 2020
    ) |> 
    inner_join(pl_2020_vars, by = "variable")
  
  subdiv_2020_dp_detail <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "dp",
      variables = dp_2020_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE, 
      year = 2020
    ) |> 
    inner_join(dp_2020_vars, by = "variable")
  
  block_2020_pl_detail <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "block",
      sumfile = "pl",
      variables = pl_2020_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE, 
      year = 2020
    ) |> 
    inner_join(pl_2020_vars, by = "variable")
  
  # dp is not available at the block level
  # block_2020_dp_detail <- 
  #   get_decennial( 
  #     geography = "block",
  #     sumfile = "dp",
  #     variables = dp_2020_vars$variable,
  #     state = "OH",
  #     county = "Cuyahoga",
  #     geometry = TRUE, 
  #     year = 2020
  #   ) |> 
  #   inner_join(dp_2020_vars, by = "variable")

  # The "_detail" objects include the original levels for race/ethnicity and age.
   
  subdiv_2020_detail <- 
    bind_rows(subdiv_2020_pl_detail, subdiv_2020_dp_detail) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      NAME = str_remove_all(NAME, "(, Cuyahoga County, Ohio)|(village)|(city)"),
      NAME = str_trim(NAME)
    ) |>
    select(-variable) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME) |>
    relocate(value, .after = var_detail)
           
  block_2020_detail <-
    block_2020_pl_detail |>
    inner_join(
      cleve_blocks |> as_tibble() |> select(GEOID20, SPA = SPA_NAME), 
      by = c("GEOID" = "GEOID20")
    ) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      greater_downtown = case_when(
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% westside_tracts_2020 ~ "Westside",
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% downtown_core_tracts_2020 ~ "Downtown Core",
        GEOID %in% downtown_core_blocks_2020 ~ "Downtown Core",
        TRUE ~ "Other"
      ),
      SPA = factor(str_to_title(SPA)),
      SPA = fct_relevel(SPA, "Downtown", after = 0),
      greater_downtown = factor(
        greater_downtown, levels = c("Downtown Core", "Westside", "Other"))
    ) |>
    select(-variable) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME) |>
    relocate(value, .after = var_detail)

  # The non-_detail objects summarize by my grouped race/ethnicity and age.
  
  subdiv_2020 <- 
    subdiv_2020_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level),
      value = sum(value)
    )
  
  block_2020 <- 
    block_2020_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level, SPA, greater_downtown),
      value = sum(value)
    )
  
  save(
    subdiv_2020_detail, block_2020_detail, subdiv_2020, block_2020, 
    file = "decennial_2020.Rdata"
  )

} else {
  
  load("decennial_2020.Rdata")
  
}


The data is sliced three ways below. The top section of the table below is the subdivision data. Cuyahoga County has 1.3 million people with Cleveland’s at 372,624. The second section groups the block-level data by SPA. The Downtown SPA had 13,302 people. This matches the data table on Cleveland Open Data. The Downtown Core defined by Urban Partners, which included portions of the Central, Ohio City, and Cuyahoga Valley SPAs, had 18,708 people.
The map on the second tab shows Cuyahoga County and all of its subdivisions. Cleveland is the largest, and each of its SPAs are broken out. The Downtown SPA is highlighted. The Urban Partners extensions to downtown aren’t shown.

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
2010
Unfortunately, pulling 2010 and 2000 isn’t as simple as changing the year parameter in the API calls because they use a different file, Summary File 1.

sf1_2010_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2010, "sf1") |>
  filter(
    concept %in% c("HISPANIC OR LATINO ORIGIN BY RACE", "SEX BY AGE"),
    !name %in% c("P005002", "P012001", "P012002", "P012026"),
    !str_detect(label, "Total!!Hispanic or Latino!!"),
    !str_detect(name, "^PCT012")
  )

I’ll prepare the variables the same way as with 2020. There are twice as many age variables this time because there is one variable for each sex.


Show the code
sf1_2010_vars <-
  sf1_2010_vars |>
  mutate(
    label = str_remove(label, "(Total!!Male!!)|(Total!!Female!!)"),
    label = str_remove(label, "(Total!!Not Hispanic or Latino!!)"),
    var_detail = case_when(
      str_detect(label, "White") ~ "White",
      str_detect(label, "Black") ~ "Black",
      str_detect(label, "Asian") ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(label, "American Indian") ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(label, "Native Hawaiian") ~ "Pacific Islander",
      str_detect(label, "Some Other Race") ~ "Other",
      str_detect(label, "Two or More Races") ~ "Two or more races",
      str_detect(label, "Hispanic") ~ "Hispanic",
      str_detect(label, "Total") ~ "Total",
      TRUE ~ label,
    ),
    var_group = case_when(
      name == "P005001" ~ "Total",
      between(name, "P005003", "P005010") ~ "Race/ethnicity",
      TRUE ~ "Age"
    ),
    var_level = case_when(
      var_detail %in% c("White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Total", "Other") ~ var_detail,
      var_detail %in% c("American Indian", "Pacific Islander", "Two or more races") ~ "Other",
      var_detail %in% c("Under 5 years", "5 to 9 years", "10 to 14 years") ~ "Under 15 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "15 to 17 years", "22 to 24 years") ~ "15 to 24 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("25 to 29 years", "30 to 34 years") ~ "25 to 34 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("35 to 39 years", "40 to 44 years") ~ "35 to 44 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("45 to 49 years", "50 to 54 years") ~ "45 to 54 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "55 to 59 years", "62 to 64 years") ~ "55 to 64 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "65 and 66 years", "85 years and over") ~ "65+ yrs"
    )
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

sf1_2010_vars

   <chr>    <chr>          <chr>        <chr>

Request the data from the API. This time I cannot join to cleve_blocks to get precise mappings of census blocks to SPAs. Instead, I’ll join to the cleve_neigh shape file to spatially join to the SPAs. This turns out to be almost as good, but not perfect.


Show the code
# Urban Partners definitions of greater Cleveland uses tracts and blocks.
westside_tracts_2010 <- westside_tracts_2020
downtown_core_tracts_2010 <- downtown_core_tracts_2020
downtown_core_blocks_2010 <- paste0("39035108701", c("3000", "3001", "3002", "3003", "3004"))

if (USE_API) {

  subdiv_2010_detail_0 <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2010_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE, 
      year = 2010
    ) |> 
    inner_join(sf1_2010_vars, by = "variable")

  subdiv_2010_detail_1 <- 
    subdiv_2010_detail_0 |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      NAME = str_remove_all(NAME, "(, Cuyahoga County, Ohio)|(village)|(city)"),
      NAME = str_trim(NAME)
    ) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME)
  
  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  subdiv_2010_detail <-
    subdiv_2010_detail_1 |> 
    select(-variable) |>
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))
           
  block_2010_detail_0 <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "block",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2010_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE, 
      year = 2010
    ) |> 
    inner_join(sf1_2010_vars, by = "variable")

  block_2010_detail_1 <-
    st_join(cleve_neigh, st_centroid(block_2010_detail_0), join = st_contains) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      greater_downtown = case_when(
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% westside_tracts_2010 ~ "Westside",
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% downtown_core_tracts_2010 ~ "Downtown Core",
        GEOID %in% downtown_core_blocks_2010 ~ "Downtown Core",
        TRUE ~ "Other"
      ),
      SPA = factor(str_to_title(SPA)),
      SPA = fct_relevel(SPA, "Downtown", after = 0),
      greater_downtown = factor(
        greater_downtown, levels = c("Downtown Core", "Westside", "Other"))
    ) |>
    select(GEOID, geo_name = NAME, geometry, var_group, var_level, var_detail, 
           value, SPA, greater_downtown)

  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  block_2010_detail <-
    block_2010_detail_1 |> 
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))
  
  subdiv_2010 <- 
    subdiv_2010_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level),
      value = sum(value)
    )
  
  block_2010 <- 
    block_2010_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level, SPA, greater_downtown),
      value = sum(value)
    )
  
  save(
    subdiv_2010_detail, block_2010_detail, subdiv_2010, block_2010, 
    file = "decennial_2010.Rdata"
  )

} else {
  
  load("decennial_2010.Rdata")
  
}


This time the sum of the neighborhoods, 395,601, doesn’t quite equal the city total, 396,815. There must be city blocks whose centers are not captured in the shapes in cleve_neigh. Comparing my values to those in the data table on Cleveland Open Data, the largest differences are in Euclid Green, Kamm’s Corners, and Hopkins. I haven’t thought of a good way to fix this, so I’m settling for “close enough”.
The Downtown SPA population of 9,464 does match the value reported in Cleveland Open Data. It was quite a bit lower than 2020 (13,302). My Downtown Core population of 15,156 is slightly different from Urban Partner’s value of 15,330. The Westside population does match though.

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
2000
2000 is similar to 2010 in that it uses Summary File 1.

sf1_2000_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2000, "sf1") |>
  filter(
    concept %in% c(
      "HISPANIC OR LATINO, AND NOT HISPANIC OR LATINO BY RACE [73]", 
      "SEX BY AGE [49]"
    ),
    !name %in% c("P004003", "P004004", "P012001", "P012002", "P012026"),
    !str_detect(label, "Population of two or more races!!"),
    !str_detect(name, "^PCT013")
  )

Same process: prepare the variables.


Show the code
sf1_2000_vars <-
  sf1_2000_vars |>
  mutate(
    label = str_remove(label, "(Total!!Male!!)|(Total!!Female!!)"),
    label = str_remove(label, "(Total!!Not Hispanic or Latino!!)"),
    var_detail = case_when(
      str_detect(label, "White") ~ "White",
      str_detect(label, "Black") ~ "Black",
      str_detect(label, "Asian") ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(label, "American Indian") ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(label, "Native Hawaiian") ~ "Pacific Islander",
      str_detect(label, "Some other race") ~ "Other",
      str_detect(label, "two or more races") ~ "Two or more races",
      str_detect(label, "Hispanic") ~ "Hispanic",
      str_detect(label, "Total") ~ "Total",
      TRUE ~ label,

    var_group = case_when(
      name == "P004001" ~ "Total",

      var_detail %in% c("25 to 29 years", "30 to 34 years") ~ "25 to 34 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("35 to 39 years", "40 to 44 years") ~ "35 to 44 yrs",

Request the data from the API. I’ll used the cleve_neigh shape file again to identify the SPAs. Tract and block identifiers can change from census to census, so I had to make some changes to the Downtown Core definition. I used the same block identifiers for Urban Partners’ definitions, but they did not include 2020 in their report, so I’m not sure how much this differs.


Show the code
# Tract definition are same for 2000.
westside_tracts_2000 <- westside_tracts_2020

downtown_core_tracts_2000 <- c(
  "107100", "107200", "107300", "107400", "107500", "107600", "107700",
  "107800", "107900", "109200")

downtown_core_blocks_2000 <- 
  paste0("39035108701", c("3000", "3001", "3002", "3003", "3004"))

if (USE_API) {

  subdiv_2000_detail_0 <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2000_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = FALSE, # no county subdivision geography in 2000
      year = 2000
    ) |> 
    inner_join(sf1_2000_vars, by = "variable") 
  
  subdiv_2000_detail_1 <- 
    subdiv_2000_detail_0 |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      NAME = str_remove_all(NAME, "(, Cuyahoga County, Ohio)|(village)|(city)"),
      NAME = str_trim(NAME)
    ) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME)

  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  subdiv_2000_detail_2 <-
    subdiv_2000_detail_1 |> 
    select(-variable) |>
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))
           
  # No geometry for 2000? No problem! Use the 2010 geometry and replace the 
  # values with 2000.
  subdiv_2000_detail_3 <- 
    subdiv_2000_detail_2 |> 
    as_tibble() |> 
    select(GEOID, var_group, var_level, var_detail, value)
  
  subdiv_2000_detail <- 
    subdiv_2010_detail |>
    select(-value) |>
    inner_join(subdiv_2000_detail_3, 
               by = c("GEOID", "var_group", "var_level", "var_detail"))

  block_2000_detail_0 <- 
    get_decennial( 
      geography = "block",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2000_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE, 
      year = 2000
    ) |> 
    inner_join(sf1_2000_vars, by = "variable")
  
  block_2000_detail_1 <-
    st_join(cleve_neigh, st_centroid(block_2000_detail_0), join = st_contains) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      greater_downtown = case_when(
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% westside_tracts_2000 ~ "Westside",
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% downtown_core_tracts_2000 ~ "Downtown Core",
        GEOID %in% downtown_core_blocks_2000 ~ "Downtown Core",
        TRUE ~ "Other"
      ),
      SPA = factor(str_to_title(SPA)),
      SPA = fct_relevel(SPA, "Downtown", after = 0),
      greater_downtown = factor(
        greater_downtown, levels = c("Downtown Core", "Westside", "Other"))
    ) |>
    select(GEOID, geo_name = NAME, geometry, var_group, var_level, var_detail, 
           value, SPA, greater_downtown)
  
  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  block_2000_detail <-
    block_2000_detail_1 |> 
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))
  
  subdiv_2000 <- 
    subdiv_2000_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level),
      value = sum(value)
    )
  
  block_2000 <- 
    block_2000_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level, SPA, greater_downtown),
      value = sum(value)
    )
  
  save(
    subdiv_2000_detail, block_2000_detail, subdiv_2000, block_2000, 
    file = "decennial_2000.Rdata"
  )

} else {
  
  load("decennial_2000.Rdata")
  
}


As with 2010, the sum of the neighborhoods, 477,107, doesn’t quite match the city value, 478,403, but that is still pretty close. Wow, 478,403 people in 2000, that’s 100K more than 2020. On the other hand, only 6,310 people lived Downtown. The Downtown resurgence of does not seem to be a recent phenomena.

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
2023 (ACS)
The 2023 American Community Survey publishes a 1-year and 5-year average. The 1-year survey might be helpful, but it doesn’t have block-level data. I’ll download the subdivision file and check in on Cleveland as a whole.

acs1_2023_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2023, "acs1") |>
  filter(
    concept %in% c("Sex by Age", "Hispanic or Latino Origin by Race"),
    # between(name, "B01001_001E_001N", "P2_011N"),
    !name %in% c("B01001_002", "B01001_026", "B03002_001", "B03002_002",
                 "B03002_010", "B03002_011"),
    name <= "B03002_012"
  )

Same variable prep.


Show the code
acs1_2023_vars <-
  acs1_2023_vars |>
  mutate(
    label = str_remove_all(label, "(Estimate!!Total:!!)|(Male:!!)|(Female:!!)"),
    var_detail = case_when(
      str_detect(label, "White") ~ "White",
      str_detect(label, "Black") ~ "Black",
      str_detect(label, "Asian") ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(label, "American Indian") ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(label, "Native Hawaiian") ~ "Pacific Islander",
      str_detect(label, "Some other race") ~ "Other",
      str_detect(label, "Two or more races") ~ "Two or more races",
      str_detect(label, "Hispanic") ~ "Hispanic",
      str_detect(label, "Total") ~ "Total",
      TRUE ~ label,
    ),
    var_group = case_when(
      name == "B01001_001" ~ "Total",
      between(name, "B03002_003", "B03002_012") ~ "Race/ethnicity",
      TRUE ~ "Age"
    ),
    var_level = case_when(
      var_detail %in% c("White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Total", "Other") ~ var_detail,
      var_detail %in% c("American Indian", "Pacific Islander", "Two or more races") ~ "Other",
      var_detail %in% c("Under 5 years", "5 to 9 years", "10 to 14 years") ~ "Under 15 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "15 to 17 years", "22 to 24 years") ~ "15 to 24 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("25 to 29 years", "30 to 34 years") ~ "25 to 34 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("35 to 39 years", "40 to 44 years") ~ "35 to 44 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("45 to 49 years", "50 to 54 years") ~ "45 to 54 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "55 to 59 years", "62 to 64 years") ~ "55 to 64 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "65 and 66 years", "85 years and over") ~ "65+ yrs"
    )
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
The resurgence of people moving to downtown Cleveland is making news.1 According to a study commissioned by Downtown Cleveland Inc., the downtown population was almost 19,000 in the 2020 census, a 22% increase from 2010.2 However, Cleveland Open Data shows only 13,0003. Cleveland Scene reports that there are lots of estimates out there, one as low as 8,000!4 What gives? The organizations may be using different sources, like the decennial US census vs the more recent, but less comprehensive, American Community Survey. But it seems more likely they are using different geographic boundaries.
I was able to reproduce some estimates. My main tools to do this were the tidycensus R package for US Census data, and the Cleveland Open Data service for Cleveland neighborhood definitions. I’ll step through the process below.

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
2020
The decennial census developer page has several data files for each census. Through trial and error, I discovered Redistricting Data (PL 94-171) contains overall population. There is a full list of variables that represent the various sub-groups of the population. I used it and the tidycensus::load_variables() function to identify the ones I want. I’ll include race/ethnicity to investigate demographic trends.

pl_2020_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2020, "pl") |>
  filter(
    between(name, "P2_001N", "P2_011N"),
    !name %in% c("P2_003N", "P2_004N")
  )

Here they are after a bit of cleaning. I created var_level to group several infrequent values into “Other”.


Show the code
pl_2020_vars <-
  pl_2020_vars |>
  mutate(
    var_detail = case_when(
      str_detect(label, "White") ~ "White",
      str_detect(label, "Black") ~ "Black",
      str_detect(label, "Asian") ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(label, "American Indian") ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(label, "Native Hawaiian") ~ "Pacific Islander",
      str_detect(label, "Some Other Race") ~ "Some Other Race",
      str_detect(label, "two or more races") ~ "Two or more races",
      str_detect(label, "Hispanic") ~ "Hispanic",
      str_detect(label, "Total") ~ "Total",
      TRUE ~ label
    ),
    var_group = if_else(name == "P2_001N", "Total", "Race/ethnicity"),
    var_level = if_else(
      var_detail %in% c("White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Total"),
      var_detail,
      "Other"
    )
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

The Demographic Profile contains age data.

dp_2020_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2020, "dp") |>
  filter(
    str_detect(label, "Count!!SEX AND AGE!!Total population"),
    !str_detect(label, "Selected Age Categories"),
    name != "DP1_0001C"
  )

I’ll ignore sex and aggregate the ages into ten-year buckets.


Show the code
dp_2020_vars <-
  dp_2020_vars |>
  mutate(
    var_detail = str_remove_all(
      label,
      "(Count!!SEX AND AGE!!Total population)|(!!)"
    ),
    var_detail = if_else(var_detail == "", "Total", var_detail),
    var_group = "Age",
    var_level = case_when(
      name <= "DP1_0004C" ~ "Under 15 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0006C" ~ "15 to 24 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0008C" ~ "25 to 34 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0010C" ~ "35 to 44 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0012C" ~ "45 to 54 yrs",
      name <= "DP1_0014C" ~ "55 to 64 yrs",
      TRUE ~ "65+ yrs"
    )
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

With the variables identified, request the data from the API. Cleveland is one of 59 subdivisions within Cuyahoga County. I’ll download the subdivision data to get a total count for Cuyahoga County and for Cleveland. Counties are composed of census tracts, and census tracts are composed of census blocks. Cities overlap census tracts, so I’ll download the block-level data and join to the cleve_blocks dataset from Cleveland Open Data. Urban Partners defined their Downtown Core and Westside areas by tract and some blocks from the Central SPA. I figured out which tracts and blocks by studying their map and swearing a lot.


Show the code
# Utility function to create factors
var_relevel <- function(x) {
  race_levels = c("Black", "White", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Other", "Total")
  ethn <- c("Black", "White", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Other", "Total")
  x <- fct_relevel(x, race_levels, after = Inf)
  x <- fct_relevel(x, "Under 15 yrs", after = 0)
  return(x)
}

# Urban Partners definitions of greater Cleveland uses tracts and blocks.
westside_tracts_2020 <- c(
  "103100",
  "103400",
  "103500",
  "103602",
  "103800",
  "103900",
  "104100",
  "104200",
  "104300",
  "197800",
  "197700",
  "197500",
  "104400"
)
downtown_core_tracts_2020 <- c("103300", "107101", "107701", "107802", "109301")
downtown_core_blocks_2020 <- paste0(
  "39035108701",
  c("2001", "2004", "2006", "2008")
)

if (USE_API) {
  subdiv_2020_pl_detail <-
    get_decennial(
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "pl",
      variables = pl_2020_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE,
      year = 2020
    ) |>
    inner_join(pl_2020_vars, by = "variable")

  subdiv_2020_dp_detail <-
    get_decennial(
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "dp",
      variables = dp_2020_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE,
      year = 2020
    ) |>
    inner_join(dp_2020_vars, by = "variable")

  block_2020_pl_detail <-
    get_decennial(
      geography = "block",
      sumfile = "pl",
      variables = pl_2020_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE,
      year = 2020
    ) |>
    inner_join(pl_2020_vars, by = "variable")

  # dp is not available at the block level
  # block_2020_dp_detail <-
  #   get_decennial(
  #     geography = "block",
  #     sumfile = "dp",
  #     variables = dp_2020_vars$variable,
  #     state = "OH",
  #     county = "Cuyahoga",
  #     geometry = TRUE,
  #     year = 2020
  #   ) |>
  #   inner_join(dp_2020_vars, by = "variable")

  # The "_detail" objects include the original levels for race/ethnicity and age.

  subdiv_2020_detail <-
    bind_rows(subdiv_2020_pl_detail, subdiv_2020_dp_detail) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      NAME = str_remove_all(NAME, "(, Cuyahoga County, Ohio)|(village)|(city)"),
      NAME = str_trim(NAME)
    ) |>
    select(-variable) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME) |>
    relocate(value, .after = var_detail)

  block_2020_detail <-
    block_2020_pl_detail |>
    inner_join(
      cleve_blocks |> as_tibble() |> select(GEOID20, SPA = SPA_NAME),
      by = c("GEOID" = "GEOID20")
    ) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      greater_downtown = case_when(
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% westside_tracts_2020 ~ "Westside",
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% downtown_core_tracts_2020 ~ "Downtown Core",
        GEOID %in% downtown_core_blocks_2020 ~ "Downtown Core",
        TRUE ~ "Other"
      ),
      SPA = factor(str_to_title(SPA)),
      SPA = fct_relevel(SPA, "Downtown", after = 0),
      greater_downtown = factor(
        greater_downtown,
        levels = c("Downtown Core", "Westside", "Other")
      )
    ) |>
    select(-variable) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME) |>
    relocate(value, .after = var_detail)

  # The non-_detail objects summarize by my grouped race/ethnicity and age.

  subdiv_2020 <-
    subdiv_2020_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level),
      value = sum(value)
    )

  block_2020 <-
    block_2020_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(
        GEOID,
        geo_name,
        geometry,
        var_group,
        var_level,
        SPA,
        greater_downtown
      ),
      value = sum(value)
    )

  save(
    subdiv_2020_detail,
    block_2020_detail,
    subdiv_2020,
    block_2020,
    file = "decennial_2020.Rdata"
  )
} else {
  load("decennial_2020.Rdata")
}

title: Cleveland’s Changing Population
text:
2010
Unfortunately, pulling 2010 and 2000 isn’t as simple as changing the year parameter in the API calls because they use a different file, Summary File 1.

sf1_2010_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2010, "sf1") |>
  filter(
    concept %in% c("HISPANIC OR LATINO ORIGIN BY RACE", "SEX BY AGE"),
    !name %in% c("P005002", "P012001", "P012002", "P012026"),
    !str_detect(label, "Total!!Hispanic or Latino!!"),
    !str_detect(name, "^PCT012")
  )

I’ll prepare the variables the same way as with 2020. There are twice as many age variables this time because there is one variable for each sex.


Show the code
sf1_2010_vars <-
  sf1_2010_vars |>
  mutate(
    label = str_remove(label, "(Total!!Male!!)|(Total!!Female!!)"),
    label = str_remove(label, "(Total!!Not Hispanic or Latino!!)"),
    var_detail = case_when(
      str_detect(label, "White") ~ "White",
      str_detect(label, "Black") ~ "Black",
      str_detect(label, "Asian") ~ "Asian",
      str_detect(label, "American Indian") ~ "American Indian",
      str_detect(label, "Native Hawaiian") ~ "Pacific Islander",
      str_detect(label, "Some Other Race") ~ "Other",
      str_detect(label, "Two or More Races") ~ "Two or more races",
      str_detect(label, "Hispanic") ~ "Hispanic",
      str_detect(label, "Total") ~ "Total",
      TRUE ~ label,
    ),
    var_group = case_when(
      name == "P005001" ~ "Total",
      between(name, "P005003", "P005010") ~ "Race/ethnicity",
      TRUE ~ "Age"
    ),
    var_level = case_when(
      var_detail %in%
        c("White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian", "Total", "Other") ~ var_detail,
      var_detail %in%
        c("American Indian", "Pacific Islander", "Two or more races") ~ "Other",
      var_detail %in%
        c("Under 5 years", "5 to 9 years", "10 to 14 years") ~ "Under 15 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "15 to 17 years", "22 to 24 years") ~ "15 to 24 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("25 to 29 years", "30 to 34 years") ~ "25 to 34 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("35 to 39 years", "40 to 44 years") ~ "35 to 44 yrs",
      var_detail %in% c("45 to 49 years", "50 to 54 years") ~ "45 to 54 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "55 to 59 years", "62 to 64 years") ~ "55 to 64 yrs",
      between(var_detail, "65 and 66 years", "85 years and over") ~ "65+ yrs"
    )
  ) |>
  select(variable = name, var_group, var_level, var_detail)

Request the data from the API. This time I cannot join to cleve_blocks to get precise mappings of census blocks to SPAs. Instead, I’ll join to the cleve_neigh shape file to spatially join to the SPAs. This turns out to be almost as good, but not perfect.


Show the code
# Urban Partners definitions of greater Cleveland uses tracts and blocks.
westside_tracts_2010 <- westside_tracts_2020
downtown_core_tracts_2010 <- downtown_core_tracts_2020
downtown_core_blocks_2010 <- paste0(
  "39035108701",
  c("3000", "3001", "3002", "3003", "3004")
)

if (USE_API) {
  subdiv_2010_detail_0 <-
    get_decennial(
      geography = "county subdivision",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2010_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE,
      year = 2010
    ) |>
    inner_join(sf1_2010_vars, by = "variable")

  subdiv_2010_detail_1 <-
    subdiv_2010_detail_0 |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      NAME = str_remove_all(NAME, "(, Cuyahoga County, Ohio)|(village)|(city)"),
      NAME = str_trim(NAME)
    ) |>
    rename(geo_name = NAME)

  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  subdiv_2010_detail <-
    subdiv_2010_detail_1 |>
    select(-variable) |>
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))

  block_2010_detail_0 <-
    get_decennial(
      geography = "block",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2010_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE,
      year = 2010
    ) |>
    inner_join(sf1_2010_vars, by = "variable")

  block_2010_detail_1 <-
    st_join(
      cleve_neigh,
      st_centroid(block_2010_detail_0),
      join = st_contains
    ) |>
    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),
      greater_downtown = case_when(
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% westside_tracts_2010 ~ "Westside",
        str_sub(GEOID, 6, 11) %in% downtown_core_tracts_2010 ~ "Downtown Core",
        GEOID %in% downtown_core_blocks_2010 ~ "Downtown Core",
        TRUE ~ "Other"
      ),
      SPA = factor(str_to_title(SPA)),
      SPA = fct_relevel(SPA, "Downtown", after = 0),
      greater_downtown = factor(
        greater_downtown,
        levels = c("Downtown Core", "Westside", "Other")
      )
    ) |>
    select(
      GEOID,
      geo_name = NAME,
      geometry,
      var_group,
      var_level,
      var_detail,
      value,
      SPA,
      greater_downtown
    )

  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  block_2010_detail <-
    block_2010_detail_1 |>
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))

  subdiv_2010 <-
    subdiv_2010_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level),
      value = sum(value)
    )

  block_2010 <-
    block_2010_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(
        GEOID,
        geo_name,
        geometry,
        var_group,
        var_level,
        SPA,
        greater_downtown
      ),
      value = sum(value)
    )

  save(
    subdiv_2010_detail,
    block_2010_detail,
    subdiv_2010,
    block_2010,
    file = "decennial_2010.Rdata"
  )
} else {
  load("decennial_2010.Rdata")
}


This time the sum of the neighborhoods, 395,601, doesn’t quite equal the city total, 396,815. There must be city blocks whose centers are not captured in the shapes in cleve_neigh. Comparing my values to those in the data table on Cleveland Open Data, the largest differences are in Euclid Green, Kamm’s Corners, and Hopkins. I haven’t thought of a good way to fix this, so I’m settling for “close enough”.
The Downtown SPA population of 9,464 does match the value reported in Cleveland Open Data. It was quite a bit lower than 2020 (13,302). My Downtown Core population of 15,156 is slightly different from Urban Partner’s value of 15,330. The Westside population does match though.

2000 is similar to 2010 in that it uses Summary File 1.

sf1_2000_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2000, "sf1") |>

  subdiv_2000_detail <-

  block_2000_detail_0 <-
    get_decennial(
      geography = "block",
      sumfile = "sf1",
      variables = sf1_2000_vars$variable,
      state = "OH",
      county = "Cuyahoga",
      geometry = TRUE,
      year = 2000
    ) |>
    inner_join(sf1_2000_vars, by = "variable")

  block_2000_detail_1 <-
    st_join(
      cleve_neigh,

    mutate(
      var_level = var_relevel(var_level),

        greater_downtown,
        levels = c("Downtown Core", "Westside", "Other")

    ) |>
    select(
      GEOID,
      geo_name = NAME,
      geometry,
      var_group,
      var_level,
      var_detail,
      value,
      SPA,
      greater_downtown
    )

  # Sum the two rows (for each sex)
  block_2000_detail <-
    block_2000_detail_1 |>
    summarize(.by = -c(value), value = sum(value))

  subdiv_2000 <-
    subdiv_2000_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(GEOID, geo_name, geometry, var_group, var_level),
      value = sum(value)
    )

  block_2000 <-
    block_2000_detail |>
    summarize(
      .by = c(
        GEOID,
        geo_name,
        geometry,
        var_group,
        var_level,
        SPA,
        greater_downtown
      ),
      value = sum(value)
    )

  save(
    subdiv_2000_detail,
    block_2000_detail,
    subdiv_2000,
    block_2000,
    file = "decennial_2000.Rdata"
  )
} else {
  load("decennial_2000.Rdata")
}

2023 (ACS)
The 2023 American Community Survey publishes a 1-year and 5-year average. The 1-year survey might be helpful, but it doesn’t have block-level data. I’ll download the subdivision file and check in on Cleveland as a whole.

acs1_2023_vars <-
  tidycensus::load_variables(2023, "acs1") |>
  filter(
    concept %in% c("Sex by Age", "Hispanic or Latino Origin by Race"),

      c(
        "B01001_002",
        "B01001_026",
        "B03002_001",
        "B03002_002",
        "B03002_010",
        "B03002_011"
      ),
    name <= "B03002_012"
  )

title: Understanding gt Tables
text:
Setup
The package comes with several sample datasets. We’ll use gtcars, a dataset of high-en sports cars, because it has a mix of numeric and categorical columns.

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

gt_cars_sample <- gt::gtcars |>
  filter(mfr %in% c("Lamborghini", "BMW")) |>
  select(mfr, model, year, hp, mpg_c, mpg_h)

dplyr::glimpse(gt_cars_sample)

Rows: 8
Columns: 6
$ mfr   <chr> "Lamborghini", "Lamborghini", "Lamborghini", "BMW", "BMW", "BMW"…
$ model <chr> "Aventador", "Huracan", "Gallardo", "6-Series", "i8", "M4", "M5"…
$ year  <dbl> 2015, 2015, 2014, 2016, 2016, 2016, 2016, 2016
$ hp    <dbl> 700, 610, 550, 315, 357, 425, 560, 560
$ mpg_c <dbl> 11, 16, 12, 20, 28, 17, 15, 15
$ mpg_h <dbl> 18, 20, 20, 30, 29, 24, 22, 22

The first thing to notice is that the object has a table body and column labels. It can potentially have a stub (row labels), header, and footer.

This is analagous to a ggplot object. There is a base plot with x and y aesthetic definitions and labs(). It’s useful to keep the analogy in mind because “gt” stands for “grammar of tables” - a loose parallel to “grammar of graphics”.






Grammar of Tables



Neither the gt site nor its GitHub README mention “grammar of tables”, but articles on the web repeatedly attribute the name to it.


Let’s first add the components that you would normally want.

text: Observations

title: Understanding gt Tables
text:
Takeaways
You now have the mental model and toolkit to create polished tables for 80% of real-world situations. We covered:

Structure: Use stubs, row groups, and column spanners to organize data logically.
Context. Use headers, footers, and footnotes to explain what readers are looking at.
Style. Use colors, text formatting, and heat maps to draw attention to what matters.

From here:

The {gt} documentation is comprehensive and you know the conceptual landscape well enough to navigate it.
Explore the ecosystem: {gtSummary} for statistical summaries, *{gtExtras}** for plots and spark lines.

title: Understanding gt Tables
text:
Build Up a Table
Let’s build up a {gt} table from scratch. Here is a reference schematic from the gt website:

The first thing to notice is that the object has a table body and column labels. It can potentially have a stub (row groups or labels), header, and footer.
This is analogous to the {ggplot} object. “gt” stands for “grammar of tables”, and just like {ggplot} layers build from a base, {gt} functions pipe together to add structure and styling.

1. The Basic Table
gt() converts a data frame into a gt_tbl object. That’s all you need, but you can optionally add tab_header(), cols_label(), tab_stubhead(), and tab_sourcenote().


Show the code
tbl_1 <- gt_cars_sample |>
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "mfr") |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Basic Table",
    subtitle = "with stub, column labels, header, and footer."
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    model = "Model",
    year = "Year",
    hp = "Horsepower",
    mpg_c = "MPG - City",
    mpg_h = "MPG - Hwy"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_stubhead("Manufacturer") |>
  gt::tab_source_note("Source: gtcars is from the gt package.")

2. Footnotes
You can add footnotes almost anywhere on the table. Base command is tab_footnote(footnote, locations) where locations is a function related to a part of the table:

cells_body()
cells_column_labels()
cells_column_spanners()
cells_grand_summary()
cells_group()
cells_row_groups()
cells_source_notes()
cells_stub()
cells_stub_grand_summary()
cells_stub_summary()
cells_stubhead()
cells_title()



Show the code
tbl_2 <- tbl_1 |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    "gt Cars is gtcars in gt.",
    locations = gt::cells_title(groups = "title")
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(
    "Horsepower over 600 is a strong vehicle.",
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = hp)
  ) |>
  # Footnote in table body. `rows` uses a formula.
  gt::tab_footnote(
    "Fuel efficient!",
    locations = gt::cells_body(
      columns = mpg_h,
      rows = mpg_h > 20
    ),
    placement = "right" # puts the marker on the right side of the cell
  ) |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Footnotes",
    subtitle = gt::md(
      "In header (`cells_title()`), <br>column labels (`cells_column_labels()`), and <br>body(`cells_body()`)"
    )
  )

3. Formatting
tab_options() is the {gt} equivalent of ggplot2::theme(). To format specific cells, use tab_tyle(style, locations) where style is a function related to a styling type:

cell_text()
cell_fill()
cell_borders()



Show the code
tbl_3 <- tbl_2 |>
  # TABLE DEFAULTS
  gt::tab_options(
    heading.align = "left",
    table.font.size = gt::pct(90)
  ) |>
  # SPECIFIC CELLS
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_fill("gray90", alpha = .5),
    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(
      columns = c(model, year, hp, mpg_c, mpg_h)
    )
  ) |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Formatting",
    subtitle = gt::md("with `tab_options()` and `tab_style()`.")
  )

4. Spanners and Groups
Column spanners group related columns together under a shared label (useful for organizing related metrics). Row groups organize rows by category.

Spanners


Show the code
tbl_4 <- tbl_3 |>
  gt::tab_spanner(
    label = "Miles per Gallon",
    columns = mpg_c:mpg_h
  ) |>
  # ALREADY CALLED THIS, BUT YOU CAN OVERRIDE THE PRIOR SETTING
  gt::cols_label(
    mpg_c = "City",
    mpg_h = "Hwy"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_fill("gray95"),
    locations = gt::cells_column_spanners()
  ) |>
  gt::tab_header(
    title = "Column Spanners",
    subtitle = "with `tab_spanner()`"
  )

tab_options() is the {gt} equivalent of ggplot2::theme(). To format specific cells, use tab_tyle(style, locations) where style is a function related to a styling type:

cell_text()
cell_fill()
cell_borders()

    locations = gt::cells_column_labels(

    )

    subtitle = gt::md("with `tab_options()` and `tab_style()`.")

title: Great Tables with gt
text:
Takeaways
You now have the mental model and toolkit to create polished tables for 80% of real-world situations. We covered:

Structure: Use stubs, row groups, and column spanners to organize data logically.
Context. Use headers, footers, and footnotes to explain what readers are looking at.
Style. Use colors, text formatting, and heat maps to draw attention to what matters.

From here:

The {gt} documentation is comprehensive and you know the conceptual landscape well enough to navigate it.
Explore the ecosystem: {gtSummary} for statistical summaries, *{gtExtras}** for plots and spark lines.

title: Tricks
text:
This page collects small, repeatable data science snippets and practical patterns I use in everyday projects. Keep it lightweight: no full story, just what works and why.



R packages: tidycensus, tigris, dplyr, sf, janitor
Always cache heavy raster/shape downloads in Rdata or .qs for speed.

library(tidycensus)
library(dplyr)
library(sf)

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# get tract geometry + ACS data (2023 ACS 1-year example)
acs <- get_acs(
  geography = 'tract',
  state = 'OH',
  county = 'Cuyahoga',
  variables = c(med_income = 'B19013_001'),
  year = 2023,
  geometry = TRUE,
  output = 'wide'
) %>%
  st_transform(5070)

Keep CRS consistent: st_transform(5070) or st_crs(3857) for mapping layers.
Null cleanup: mutate(med_income = if_else(estimate_med_income < 0, NA_real_, estimate_med_income)).





Start with gt(df) and use pipes.
Highlight numeric ranges or outliers with data_color().
Use cols_label() to make informative headers.
tab_style with cell_text(weight = 'bold') on interesting rows.

table <- mtcars %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column('model') %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_label(
    mpg = 'MPG',
    cyl = 'Cylinders',
    hp = 'Horsepower'
  ) %>%
  data_color(
    columns = vars(mpg),
    colors = scales::col_numeric(c('white', 'blue'), domain = NULL)
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = vars(mpg, hp), decimals = 1)

# save as image
gtsave(table, 'gt-mtcars.png')




Use chunk options to keep pages readable:

echo: false for finished results
eval: false for illustrative snippets
collapse: true for long pipelines

Link content with relative paths:

[Notes](../notes/index.qmd)
[Experiments](../experiments/index.qmd)

For reproducible output, freeze dependencies with Quarto site format and lock R package versions via {renv}.





clean names + type conversions:

library(dplyr)
library(janitor)

df <- data %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character), str_trim)) %>%
  mutate(across(starts_with('date'), as.Date))

pivoting census variables:

wide <- acs %>%
  select(GEOID, variable, estimate) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = estimate)




Check package versions with sessionInfo().
Saving intermediate outputs:

saveRDS(acs, 'data/acs_cuyahoga_2023.rds')

When HTML/table layout breaks, clear Quarto cache: quarto render --no-cache.





tidycensus::load_variables(year, 'acs1', cache = TRUE)
tigris::counties(state = 'OH', cb = TRUE)
sf::st_intersection() for spatial overlays
gt::glue_data() for dynamic cell text





Keep this file updated with tricks that save at least 5 minutes per task.
Put full experimental explorations in experiments/, and keep notes/tricks.qmd as your short-direct-action cookbook.

title: LLM Providers
text: An analyst should understand the landscape of LLM providers to make informed decisions about which models. Pay attention on operational and practical aspects that affect your work. Here’s what matters most.

title: LLM Providers
text:
Survey of Major LLM Providers
Let’s briefly survey the major LLM providers.

Model DevelopersPlatform/Cloud DistributorsTools/Utilities


Companies that primarily build and train their own LLMs.

Anthropic (Claude)DeepSeekGoogle Gemini/Vertex AIMistralOpenAI


Who They Are: Founded by ex-OpenAI researchers, Anthropic focuses on building safe, beneficial AI. They’re known for prioritizing AI safety and ethical development.
Why Pay Attention: Rapidly growing with major partnerships (Amazon, Google, Microsoft), they’re pushing boundaries in AI safety and reasoning capabilities. Their models are frequently updated and competitive in benchmarks.
Capabilities for Summarization: Claude models offer some of the longest context windows in the industry and excel at complex reasoning and multi-step analysis, making them strong for nuanced qualitative data summarization.
Pricing & Integration: Competitive per-token pricing with batch processing discounts. Strong API reliability and enterprise features.
Data Privacy: Enterprise-grade privacy with US-only inference options; no data training without opt-out.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Best-in-class for agentic workflows and complex tasks; available via major cloud platforms (AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex, Microsoft Foundry) for multi-cloud deployments.
Business Model & Positioning: Anthropic monetizes through API consumption (per-token pricing), consumer subscriptions, enterprise licensing, and partnerships with cloud providers. Strong competitive advantage in reasoning/extended thinking capabilities and enterprise safety focus; positioned as premium provider targeting regulated industries and complex reasoning tasks.


Who They Are: Chinese AI startup focused on efficient, open-source models.
Why Pay Attention: Offers exceptional value with strong reasoning at low cost; growing rapidly.
Capabilities for Summarization: Models with good context windows and reasoning, competitive with premium providers.
Pricing: Significantly cheaper than OpenAI (~90% less).
Data Privacy: China-based; may not suit US/EU compliance needs.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Open-source focus with aggregator availability; good for budget teams.
Business Model & Positioning: DeepSeek (China-based) monetizes through freemium models with API consumption (~$0.27/$0.40 per million tokens for V3.2—80-90% cheaper than OpenAI). Market positioning: exceptional cost-to-capability ratio targeting budget-conscious teams, strong reasoning via distilled R1 models; disadvantage for US/EU enterprises requiring data sovereignty and compliance guarantees.


Who They Are: Google’s AI division, leveraging DeepMind research, offering models through Vertex AI platform.
Why Pay Attention: Backed by Google’s resources and research, they provide multimodal and multilingual capabilities with strong cloud integrations.
Capabilities for Summarization: Gemini models support multimodal input (text + images) and excel in multilingual tasks, useful for diverse data summarization.
Pricing: Competitive per-token rates.
Data Privacy: Strong Google Cloud compliance (SOC 2, HIPAA-eligible).
Scalability & Ecosystem: Deep GCP integrations (BigQuery, etc.); great for Google-centric workflows.
Business Model & Positioning: Google monetizes through per-API token consumption ($1.25-$15/M input tokens) and Vertex AI enterprise services with commitments; leverages Google Cloud infrastructure for customer lock-in. Competitive strengths: multimodal capabilities, 100+ language support, integration with BigQuery/workbenches, and backing by DeepMind research for continuous capability upgrades.


Who They Are: European AI company focused on open-source models.
Why Pay Attention: Strong on European languages and privacy compliance.
Capabilities for Summarization: Good context and multilingual support.
Pricing: Competitive rates.
Data Privacy: EU-based with GDPR focus.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Open-source options; enterprise deployments.
Business Model & Positioning: Mistral (EU-based) monetizes through API consumption (~$0.30-$2/M tokens), enterprise licensing, and custom model development services (Forge, advanced R&D). Market positioning: EU data residency compliance with GDPR enforcement, open-source model availability for private deployment, and strong European enterprise focus; advantage for regulations-sensitive organizations.


Who They Are: Founded by Elon Musk and a team of AI researchers, OpenAI is a leading independent AI company focused on advancing AGI (artificial general intelligence) safely.
Why Pay Attention: As pioneers in generative AI, they have massive influence, frequent model updates, and widespread adoption across industries. Their models set industry standards.
Capabilities for Summarization: GPT family models excel at complex reasoning and multi-step tasks, making them reliable for nuanced qualitative data summarization.
Pricing: Premium per-token rates, reflecting their cutting-edge performance.
Data Privacy: Zero data retention by default; strong compliance options (SOC 2, HIPAA).
Scalability & Ecosystem: Mature ecosystem with extensive documentation and integrations; ideal for production deployments despite higher costs.
Business Model & Positioning: OpenAI monetizes through: (1) ChatGPT subscriptions ($20+/month consumer), (2) API token consumption ($5-$25/M tokens depending on model tier), (3) enterprise licensing/agreements (Department of War contract, Amazon partnership). Market positioning: frontier model performance (GPT-5 series), industry-standard API, and brand reputation; disadvantage: premium pricing and vendor lock-in risk.





Companies that host, integrate, or resell models from developers, often with added infrastructure or enterprise features.

AWS BedrockAzure OpenAICloudflareDatabricksGitHub Model MarketplaceGroqHugging FaceOpenRouterSnowflake Cortex


Who They Are: Amazon’s managed service for accessing various foundation models.
Why Pay Attention: Offers a wide range of models with strong enterprise features and AWS integrations.
Capabilities for Summarization: Access to top models like Claude and Llama; good for benchmarking and optimization.
Pricing: Pay-as-you-go or provisioned throughput.
Data Privacy: Strong compliance (HIPAA, SOC 2); no training on customer data.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Deep AWS integrations; excellent for large-scale, multi-model deployments.
Business Model & Positioning: Amazon’s multi-model platform monetizes through pay-per-use token consumption and provisioned throughput commitments; generates revenue while strengthening AWS ecosystem lock-in. Competitive strength lies in model diversity (100+ models), deep AWS integration, compliance certifications (HIPAA, FedRAMP), and cost optimization for enterprise customers at scale.


Who They Are: Microsoft’s cloud service offering exclusive access to OpenAI’s models within the Azure ecosystem.
Why Pay Attention: Combines OpenAI’s cutting-edge models with Microsoft’s enterprise infrastructure, compliance, and integrations—ideal for large organizations.
Capabilities for Summarization: Access to GPT family models with strong analytical performance, suitable for financial and qualitative data summarization.
Pricing: Provisioned throughput tied to Azure consumption (enterprise-focused).
Data Privacy: Top-tier compliance (HIPAA, FedRAMP) with regional data residency and encryption.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Seamless Microsoft 365/Power BI integrations and governance tools; best for Microsoft-centric enterprises.
Business Model & Positioning: Microsoft’s exclusive Azure distribution of OpenAI’s models generates revenue through provisioned/standard throughput pricing tied to Azure account consumption. Key differentiators: Microsoft 365 integration, priority access to latest models, regional data residency compliance, and bundling with Azure AI services for enterprises using Microsoft tech stacks.


Who They Are: Cloud infrastructure company expanding into AI with edge-deployed models.
Why Pay Attention: Focuses on low-latency, global inference for fast, accessible AI.
Capabilities for Summarization: Supports various open models with good context; cost-effective for moderate workloads.
Pricing: Flexible, often included in plans.
Data Privacy: Edge deployment minimizes data transfer.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Global POPs for low latency; integrates with Cloudflare tools.
Business Model & Positioning: Cloudflare monetizes LLM inference through edge-hosted models across 200+ POPs; included in Workers Pro plans or priced per-token for consumption. Unique positioning: ultra-low latency inference at the edge, developer-friendly integration, and data residency guarantees; competitive advantage for real-time, latency-sensitive applications.


Who They Are: Data platform company offering AI integrations in their lakehouse.
Why Pay Attention: Seamlessly combines data processing with AI for analysts.
Capabilities for Summarization: Native SQL/Python functions for summarization without data movement.
Pricing: Consumption-based on platform usage.
Data Privacy: Data stays in your lakehouse/VPC.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Integrates with Spark/Delta; ideal for Databricks users.
Business Model & Positioning: Databricks generates revenue through consumption-based pricing on its unified lakehouse platform (DBU/DSU units) for data storage, compute, and LLM serving integrated with data pipelines. Competitive advantage: data stays in lakehouse with native governance, seamless Spark/Delta Lake integration, and end-to-end lineage for analysts already invested in Databricks ecosystem.


Who They Are: GitHub’s emerging AI model hosting service.
Why Pay Attention: Convenient for developers already on GitHub.
Capabilities for Summarization: Access to models like Llama and Mistral.
Pricing: Mixed, tied to GitHub Enterprise.
Data Privacy: Standard GitHub policies.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Integrates with Copilot; early-stage.
Business Model & Positioning: GitHub monetizes model serving through GitHub Enterprise integrations and potential future revenue-sharing with model creators; currently early-stage with limited API infrastructure. Positioning: convenient for GitHub-native developer workflows with Copilot integration; disadvantages include unclear long-term SLA commitments and limited model diversity compared to competitors.


Who They Are: Hardware-focused startup with custom AI chips for fast inference.
Why Pay Attention: Pioneers in low-latency AI, offering speed advantages for real-time tasks.
Capabilities for Summarization: Supports fast models like Llama; ideal for quick, iterative summarization.
Pricing: Low-cost token rates.
Data Privacy: No training on user data.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Best for latency-critical workflows; limited model variety.
Business Model & Positioning: Groq monetizes through GroqCloud consumption (per-token pricing) leveraging its proprietary LPU (Language Processing Unit) hardware; recently raised $750M for scaling. Unique differentiator: 10x faster inference throughput via custom silicon; competitive advantage for latency-critical applications (real-time analytics, streaming); disadvantage: model diversity limited vs. GPU-based competitors.


Who They Are: Open-source AI community platform with model hosting.
Why Pay Attention: Massive ecosystem for experimenting with diverse models.
Capabilities for Summarization: 2M+ models, including specialists; good for research.
Pricing: Free tier + metered for compute.
Data Privacy: Depends on provider; open-source options for local use.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Community-driven; flexible for prototyping.
Business Model & Positioning: Hugging Face monetizes through: (1) Inference Endpoints compute ($0.60+/hour), (2) Inference API token consumption via partner providers ($0.02-$1.48/M tokens), (3) Enterprise Team/HF Compute plans ($20+/user/month). Strengths: massive open-source ecosystem (2M+ models), research community engagement, flexibility to run models locally; positioning as community-first, with enterprise services layered on top.


Who They Are: Aggregator providing unified access to multiple AI providers.
Why Pay Attention: Simplifies experimenting across providers without switching APIs.
Capabilities for Summarization: Routes to top models for cost/latency optimization.
Pricing: Variable, based on underlying providers.
Data Privacy: Inherits from chosen provider.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Fallback routing for reliability; no lock-in.
Business Model & Positioning: OpenRouter monetizes through margin on aggregated provider pricing (pay-as-you-go credits, no subscriptions); acts as a marketplace arbitrageur distributing requests across 60+ providers/300+ models. Strengths: price comparison tools, multi-provider uptime optimization, no vendor lock-in; positioning as neutral aggregator for cost optimization and reliability-conscious teams.


Who They Are: Snowflake’s AI features integrated into their data warehouse.
Why Pay Attention: Keeps data in-warehouse for seamless AI workflows.
Capabilities for Summarization: SQL functions for direct summarization.
Pricing: Tied to Snowflake consumption.
Data Privacy: Data stays secure in warehouse.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Ideal for Snowflake users; no data movement.
Business Model & Positioning: Snowflake monetizes through consumption-based pricing on Cortex AI functions within Snowflake warehouse credits, alongside data storage/compute. Competitive advantage: data stays in secure warehouse perimeter with native governance, seamless SQL integration (SUMMARIZE() functions), and unified structured/unstructured data analysis—optimal for existing Snowflake customers.





Open-source or specialized tools for running or integrating models, without core development.

Ollamaperplexity.aiVLLM


Who They Are: Open-source tool for local AI model inference.
Why Pay Attention: Enables privacy-focused, offline AI without cloud costs.
Capabilities for Summarization: Runs various open models locally.
Pricing: Free and open-source.
Data Privacy: Complete local control; no cloud exposure.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Scales to your hardware; community integrations.
Business Model & Positioning: Ollama monetizes through free open-source software with potential cloud hardware upsells (faster inference via cloud account); future monetization via premium features and managed services announced. Positioning: local-first inference for data privacy, 40K+ integrations with dev tools; advantage for teams avoiding cloud dependencies; disadvantage: limited SLAs and production-grade support.


Who They Are: AI search engine combining LLMs with real-time web data.
Why Pay Attention: Adds web search for up-to-date, research-oriented summarization.
Capabilities for Summarization: Hybrid LLM + search for current events.
Pricing: Subscription-based.
Data Privacy: Processes queries; review policies.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Best for knowledge workers; limited API.
Business Model & Positioning: Perplexity monetizes through subscription tiers (~$20/month Pro) combining LLM + real-time web search; limited API availability (currently not production-ready for enterprise pipelines). Positioning: specialized for knowledge workers needing web-aware summarization; disadvantage for data analysts: limited API, primarily consumer/knowledge-work focused.


Who They Are: Open-source library for efficient model inference.
Why Pay Attention: Enables cost-effective, self-hosted AI at scale.
Capabilities for Summarization: Optimizes throughput for various models.
Pricing: Free and open-source.
Data Privacy: Runs on your infrastructure.
Scalability & Ecosystem: Supports diverse hardware; high throughput.
Business Model & Positioning: VLLM (open-source) monetizes indirectly through adoption by hosting providers (RunPod, Lambda, AWS Neuron, etc.) who charge for compute; PyTorch Foundation backing supports development. Strengths: highest throughput optimization via PagedAttention, supports all major hardware (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, TPU, CPU), 74K+ GitHub stars; positioning as foundation for self-hosted, cost-optimized inference at scale.

title: LLM Providers
text:
Categorizing the Landscape
{ellmer} 0.4.0 supports 17 providers. That’s a great list to understand the LLM landscape. We can group them into three categories based on their primary role:

Model Developers
Companies that primarily build and train their own LLMs.

Anthropic (Claude)
DeepSeek
Google (Gemini)
Mistral
OpenAI (GPT)



Platform/Cloud Distributors
Companies that host, integrate, or resell models from developers, often with added infrastructure or enterprise features.

AWS Bedrock
Azure OpenAI
Cloudflare
Databricks
GitHub Model Marketplace
Groq
Hugging Face
OpenRouter
Snowflake Cortex



Tools/Utilities
Open-source or specialized tools for running or integrating models, without core development.

Ollama
perplexity.ai
VLLM

Think of this breakdown as: Developers for innovation, Distributors for accessibility, Tools for flexibility/privacy.
